title: Terminal Warrior
learning_objectives: Navigate the Linux filesystem with speed and accuracy
Discover hidden files and analyse file permissions
Build command pipelines for security-relevant tasks
scenario: Your mentor has set up a challenge on your Kali VM. Navigate the filesystem, find hidden files, decode a message, and prove you can operate in the terminal under time pressure. Speed and accuracy both count.

scenario_context.setting: Kali Linux VM with planted challenge files
scenario_context.challenge: Filesystem navigation and discovery under time pressure
scenario_context.relevance: Every offensive engagement begins and ends in the terminal. Speed and comfort with the CLI directly impacts operational effectiveness.

subtasks.title: Filesystem Navigation Challenge
subtasks.description: Navigate to 5 system directories (/etc, /var/log, /tmp, /opt, /usr/share). List contents and document 3 interesting files in each.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Use ls -la in each directory. For 'interesting' files, look for configuration files, logs with data, scripts, and anything security-relevant. Explain why each file is interesting from an attacker's perspective.
subtasks.evidence_required: Terminal output showing navigation and file discovery

subtasks.title: Hidden File Hunt
subtasks.description: Find all hidden files in /home and /tmp. Read contents and document anything resembling credentials or configuration data.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Use ls -la and find to locate dotfiles. Read contents with cat. Look for .bash_history, .ssh directories, .env files, cached credentials. Document everything found.
subtasks.evidence_required: List of hidden files found with contents summary

subtasks.title: Permission Analysis
subtasks.description: Find 5 files with unusual permissions (world-writable, SUID, etc.) and explain each security concern.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Use find to locate SUID binaries (find / -perm -4000), world-writable files (find / -perm -002), and files owned by root but writable by others. Explain the security risk of each.
subtasks.evidence_required: Permission analysis table: file, permissions, concern

subtasks.title: Command Chain Challenge
subtasks.description: Write a single command pipeline that finds all .conf files modified in the last 7 days, searches them for 'password', and saves results to a file.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Combine find, xargs/exec, grep, and output redirection into a single pipeline. Test it. Make sure it handles filenames with spaces. The goal is efficiency and correctness.

reflection_prompt: You gain a shell on a target system during an engagement. You have 5 minutes before the connection might be detected. What are the first 10 commands you run, and why?

quiz.question: SUID binaries are security-relevant because:
options: They execute with the file owner's privileges regardless of who runs them
quiz.explanation: SUID binaries run with the permissions of the file owner (often root), making them potential privilege escalation vectors.

quiz.question: The command 'find / -perm -002' searches for:
quiz.explanation: The -002 permission flag matches files that are writable by 'others' (world-writable), which can be a security risk.

mentor_notes: Speed matters but accuracy matters more. Check that the student understands WHY each finding is security-relevant, not just that they found it. The command chain in subtask 4 should be elegant and functional.
